Answer: ⓐ The sequence is arithmetic with common difference $$d = 11$$ . ⓑ The sequence is arithmetic with common difference $$d = -6$$ . ⓒ The sequence is not arithmetic as all the differences between the consecutive terms are not the same.

To decide whether a sequence is arithmetic, subtract each term from the term that follows it.  
If the differences are all identical, the sequence is arithmetic and that repeated value is the common difference d.

(a) 9, 20, 31, 42, 53, 64, …  
 20 − 9 = 11  
 31 − 20 = 11  
 42 − 31 = 11  
 53 − 42 = 11  
 64 − 53 = 11  

 All differences equal 11 ⇒ arithmetic with common difference d = 11.

(b) 12, 6, 0, −6, −12, −18, …  
 6 − 12 = −6  
 0 − 6 = −6  
 −6 − 0 = −6  
 −12 − (−6) = −6  
 −18 − (−12) = −6  

 All differences equal −6 ⇒ arithmetic with common difference d = −6.

(c) 7, 1, 10, 4, 13, 7, …  
 1 − 7 = −6  
 10 − 1 = 9  
 4 − 10 = −6  
 13 − 4 = 9  
 7 − 13 = −6  

 Differences alternate between −6 and 9 ⇒ not constant ⇒ not an arithmetic sequence.

Summary  
(a) Arithmetic, d = 11  
(b) Arithmetic, d = −6  
(c) Not arithmetic
